sentences.text: No.
I don't know where they went, because they also went into hiding because the Germans came and they could not go back to their home.
I don't know.
I never found out where they went or what happened to them.
That was the-- in Vic-sur-Cere.
Before I left Vic-sur-Cere--
--I was given the papers with Elise [INAUDIBLE].
Yes.
She only-- I do remember it-- we did not write very often to each other because we could not say what we wanted anyway.
But just-- she wanted me to know that she was all right, as far as it went.
And she-- the last postcard I received from my mother was when she wrote to me that she was in Drancy and that she was waiting for my father.
And this is the last time I heard from my mother.
And just about-- maybe a little bit before, I received a postcard from my father saying that he hopes to see my mother very soon.
I don't know if they ever did.
I don't believe so.
No, that was when I was in Vic-sur-Cere.
Yes.
Well, this is actually the only thing, you know?
That we were with young people.
We were part of a group.
And there, we-- they fed us and clothed us.
And it was something so special for us.
And-- but other than that, there was nothing extraordinary.
I remember laying in bed at night, looking out and-- and hoping that I would see my parents.
This I remember very well because there-- there were many nights when I couldn't sleep too well.
Even so, I was very active during the day.
But this, of course, is-- was always closest, you know, to me, and the most important, that-- what will happen to my parents?
And because in some instances, I do know some children who were in different Girl Guides or camps had-- were returned to Rivesaltes and were deported from there.
So there was always that fear that it would happen to us.
But we were-- we were fortunate.
I was-- somewhat, yes, after the camp.
I mean, I was always, as a child, thin.
And of course, in camp, we had nothing to eat, or practically nothing.
I was very thin.
Yes, I must have gained some weight in the Girl Guides because they really were very good to us.
They even-- they gave us chocolate, which was rationed in those days.
They couldn't get very much themselves.
But we-- they were terrific.
I was ill, but nothing serious.
I didn't have any serious illnesses.
I did suffer for a while with throat infections and things like this.
But it was nothing-- things that-- that passed.
Just as long as we had a toothbrush.
But the toothpaste we didn't have after a while.
And I had-- I've had problems ever since with my gums and teeth.
I'm every few months at the dentist and-- but so far, I had some surgery on-- gum surgery and things like this-- I suppose from malnutrition and all these things.
It-- in later years, it shows up.
No, it did not happen.
No, much later.
I got that money-- the-- the stamp and-- from, when I was in the Girl Guides camp-- from that couple that took care of us.
They gave-- I gave them the-- the postcard and they sent it for me.
When I was in Vic-sur-Cere, the same thing happened there.
But soon after, I didn't hear from my parents anymore.
There was no correspondence.
Yes.
No, I just-- we just wore what we had.
I wasn't that heavy.
I did gain a little.
But I was not that heavy.
But I still had that gray coat that I took with me from home, which was much too small by that time because I was already about 16, maybe, at that time, and-- but I-- I was-- I still wore that.
I had nothing else.
Nothing.
Nothing at all.
Nothing.
Actually, from the Girl Guides camp-- I just remember.
We went to Leon with Rachel Hirsch, who had a cousin living there by the name of Edgar Miller.
And she gave the name and address to that couple where we stayed with at the Girl Guides.
And they contacted Edgar Miller.
And they said yes, we could come to Leon for a short time.
He had several children and his wife.
And they had only-- it was a one-bedroom apartment.
And Rachel and I, we shared a bed there.
And we were there-- he was hiding us there because we did not go to the-- into the street because at that time, the Germans were there.
And he instructed us if someone at night comes to the door, there was a window in one-- in the bedroom where we were too, and some kind of a rope to hold on to go down, to disappear.
Fortunately, we didn't have to do that.
And-- but we were there a short time because it became too dangerous for him to keep us.
And he had a family, after all.
And he was also-- since they rounded up the French Jews, he was in danger also.
He contacted the OSE.
And they arranged, then, for Rachel and I to go to Vic-sur-Cere.
This is how we got there.
Yes.
We left the Girl Guides camp in August 1942.
We were from July to August in the-- the Girl Guides camp.
And then we went to Leon August 28th, 1942.
We were there until December the 2nd, 1942.
And we went to Vic-sur-Cere.
It was just-- it was-- we were acquaintances.
No, I never-- she was not the type of person you could get very close to.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Yes.
By train.
We-- we were given the train tickets.
And Rachel and I, we went by ourselves.
And we were picked up at the station by the matron of the home where we stayed.
I don't recall.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] I don't recall how long it was.
It is in the-- it's Cantal.
It's-- the province is called Cantal.
It is-- yes.
And it is not far from, I believe, Clermont-Ferrand.
It's the next-biggest city from there.
And it was a very pretty surrounding.
We were in that home.
There were a lot of young people from all parts of the world.
There were some young people there from-- who were born in Hungary.
And-- and I met Margot Myer there again, that-- I-- I knew her from childhood.
And Amelie Myer I met there again.
We met on the steps one-- the-- the first morning I was there in Vic-sur-Cere.
No, she was from Pforzheim, but we went to the same school.
But our parents knew-- they knew each other very well, because her mother came from Konigsbach.
It was wonderful to see a familiar face.
It was-- and we were together from then on.
Just the three of us.
Just the three of us.
We were together for a number of years.
We-- when they left Vic-sur-Cere, they said they will see to-- to get a family in the same town, in Saint-Jean, where I could work so we could be together.
And they did that.
And the OSE, they provided the financial, you know, help to-- for me to get-- go there.
Yes.
Yes.
Oh, yes, we did.
We were always together, yes.
We all-- we remained friends until I left Vic-sur-Cere.
And she went later on-- or she had planned to go to Switzerland.
But of course, she couldn't go at that time because of the situation.
And there were-- one couldn't travel.
And the-- the Swiss wouldn't let any Jews in.
And-- but she found, in a small place, her mother.
Her mother was alive.
And later on, I understand she went with her mother to Israel.
I was frightened.
I didn't know what will happen from one day to the next.
And it is so devastating, when you one day find yourself alone.
You don't know.
You don't have anything.
You don't know where to go or what to do.
And you are dependent on some total strangers and-- as I said, except for Margot and Amelie, they they were, you know-- they were my support.
And we muddled through together.
And it was-- but it was very difficult.
It is so difficult, you cannot hardly put it into words.
You didn't think that way in those days.
You couldn't because you didn't know whether they would come the next day to deport you.
You-- you couldn't-- we had-- we lived from day to day.
We did not even dare to hope.
It's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were all three of us clinging to each other for support.
Well, I was asked to work in the house, to help it keep clean.
So I scrubbed the stairs, and I-- and I cleaned the-- the matron-- or the directress's-- her office, and some other offices that people that-- who run the place.
And I took care of that.
And when I was finished with that, I had to help in the kitchen.
And when I was finished with that, I had to help doing the laundry.
And it was bitter cold outside.
It was so cold that I had frostbites very often on my hands because the cold water.
We had to do everything with cold water.
And so this is what I did.
And in the evening, I was happy when I was inside.
It-- I did have a bed.
And I shared a bed with Amelie.
And so we-- we were together.
And so we-- we did the best we could.
No.
They did give us some clothing, you know, whatever they had.
There was not much available.
So we just wore whatever we had.
And that's all.
No.
I-- I ate with the others.
I didn't get anything extra or-- no, I didn't.
It was-- we had bread in the afternoon.
About 4 o'clock, we used to get-- with a little jam on it.
And we had the-- there was no meat or anything like this because everything was rationed to begin with.
We had potatoes [INAUDIBLE], and we were perfectly happy as long as we had something to eat.
But it was-- that was the least of our concern, you know?
It's-- because we-- we didn't go hungry.
We didn't-- we weren't awake at night like we were in the camps, because we couldn't sleep because we were so hungry.
How were you wearing your hair in those days?
I had it longer.
But then it was cut shorter because of the-- you know, the lice [INAUDIBLE] to get rid of it and all this.

But only later on, when I was in-- when I was liberated-- or when I was free-- let's say when I was working in-- with people in France-- I let it grow a little bit longer because I didn't have the money to have my hair cut.
So I left it long.
And I pulled it up so it should look neat.
They were also-- Margot was very good in sewing.
So the-- the director of the home, she gave her things to sew for herself.
And-- and Amelie, she didn't do too much.
She helped wherever she was asked to help.
In Vic-sur-Cere, I was until February 1944.
So I was there from December '42 to February '44.
There, we received the false papers [INAUDIBLE] with our names.
Our names were changed.
Mine was Elise [INAUDIBLE].
And because we had to travel-- and the Germans were coming, so we had to leave.
The-- the-- the home was liquidated.
And everybody was sent to different places.
Amelie-- as I said before, Emily and-- and Margot, they went to work for people in Saint-Jean-en-Royans, it was called-- a small place.
And they got a family through the people where they worked for me to go there to work to take care of their children, and the cleaning, and-- and so on.
And that home, or the OSE, I suppose, gave them the money to-- for me to travel.
And this is when we got the papers.
The director of that home took care of it.
And there was a-- a priest by the name of Glasberg, who was originally Jewish.
And he went-- I understand when he was very young, he changed his religion to Catholicism.
And he was-- but from what I have read about him, he never denied his origin as being a Jew, born as a Jew.
And he was helpful in procuring the papers with the director of the home.
And this is how it came about.
I never met him.
I only heard about him.
No.
That's the only thing.
I saw an obit in a-- or someone sent me a-- a copy of the obit that-- when he died.
And it was mentioned in there about the home.
And it was mentioned that he was born as a Jew and that--
That's the only thing.
Yes, yes.
They got-- received the same thing.
I don't recall their names, what their names were at-- I don't recall that.
No.
No one else.
That is, the people, of course where we worked for knew that we were Jewish.
No, none of them were.
There were no Jewish families in that small town.
And they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took us in to work for them.
And of course, they were at risk also, because if the Germans would have found out-- I remember when the family that I worked for said to me one day-- there was the kitchen.
And there was-- they had three children, three little girls.
And one, the baby, was in the playpen.
And so the lady said to me, if the Germans ever walk in or anybody walks in and asks you questions, make yourself very small.
Go inside with Michelle to the-- in the playpen and make yourself even smaller, you know?
As small as you can-- not to-- you know, they shouldn't ask you where you come from, where-- any of this.
But it didn't happen.
So it-- the Germans did come.
And we were bombed.
At night, we were bombed by the Americans, and during the day, by the Germans.
And we were-- once, I remember I was running through the shelter and for out-- that was outside the house, and a bomb went right over my head.
And I could see it so clearly.
And it-- it exploded a little bit away-- quite a bit away, I should say.
But it was scary to see it right over your head.
And so-- and then, when I were-- we were in Saint-Jean.
That was already later.
And you could see it-- it's going towards the end of the war.
And there was a lot of underground movement going on.
And there was a Dr.
Almen who used to come to the people where I worked.
And it's-- I believe the man where I worked, he was participating in all of these activities.
And he-- but the Germans caught him and executed him.
Dr.
Almen.
And then, as the war close-- got-- was close to the end, they caught some Germans and they hanged them in the village square.
And I remember Amelie and Margot saying that I should come and watch, but I-- but I couldn't do that.
I-- I didn't and I couldn't-- and I couldn't do it.
And I remember that so well.
And then there was that family that market worked for, where the husband was a doctor.
He was in the marines and-- a doctor in the marines.
And his wife and children were-- one child at that time-- were in Saint-Jean because they lived in Toulon.
Ans as the war drew closer, they-- they said they were gonna return to Toulon, and would Margot go with them, and they would need someone else.
Would I come along?
Amelie had left already to some other part-- I don't remember where she went.
And I didn't want to be alone in a small village.
And I had no place-- no one there.
But I must add, before I-- we went to Toulon, when the Germans came to Saint-Jean, and-- the people I worked for were afraid.
And they took me to a convent.
And I was there for a while-- not too long.
But if-- OK, I had to learn to pray in French and this.
I did all this.
But in my heart, in the evening, I did my own prayers.
But I remember it from home-- by-- by heart.

But if those people wouldn't have been satisfied with the work I did for them, I would still be there, and nobody would have ever known that I was alive, because there was no one there to-- to say, well, she's in the convent or she was hidden there.
So in that respect, I was fortunate that they took me out again.
It's-- it's a very small place.
Saint-Jean-en-Royans, it was called.
I don't even know what the next-biggest city was.
It was in-- in the country somewhere, a small area.
I don't recall.
I-- I-- no.
No, it wasn't in the south of France because the winter were quite severe, I remember.
But I-- I don't-- I don't know exactly where-- where that was, which area.
No.
I had food.
That's all.
No.
No, I had my own room.
I did, yes.
No.
No.
I wanted to learn French.
And I picked up from the children whatever I could.
I never spoke German to anyone.
